description: Bright red blood in vomit (hematemesis) in a German Shepherd with distended abdomen
notes: Hematemesis + distended abdomen in deep-chested breed = GDV/bloat with gastric necrosis until proven otherwise. Surgical emergency. Never recommend waiting or monitoring.

description: Yellow bile vomit on an empty stomach, occurs before breakfast in a Poodle
notes: Bilious vomiting syndrome is common and often managed with a late-night snack. However, in Poodles, must rule out Addison's disease (breed predisposition). Ask about other symptoms.

description: Partially digested food shortly after eating in a Labrador
notes: Regurgitation vs vomiting distinction is important. If food is undigested and happens immediately, may be regurgitation (esophageal issue). If digested, true vomiting. Monitor for recurrence.

description: Foreign object (toy piece) visible in vomit in a Golden Retriever puppy
notes: Even if foreign object was vomited, pieces may remain in GI tract. Must monitor for continued vomiting, abdominal pain, or lethargy. Puppies are high risk for obstruction.

description: White foam vomit, occasional, in a Pug with coughing
notes: White foam + coughing in a brachycephalic breed: could be kennel cough, BAOS, or tracheal collapse. The 'vomiting' may actually be post-tussive gagging. Must assess breathing quality.

description: Coffee-ground appearance vomit (digested blood) in a senior Rottweiler
notes: Coffee-ground vomit = digested blood from upper GI tract. In a senior Rottweiler, consider gastric carcinoma, ulcer, or coagulopathy. This is an emergency requiring immediate evaluation.
